courseProfile.summary: Classic Open links. Driving control, penetrating iron play and a sharp short game in the wind separate the field. Putting matters least of the four here - ball-striking and scrambling win Opens.
courseProfile.narrative: Classic Open links. Driving control, penetrating iron play and a sharp short game in the wind separate the field. Putting matters least of the four here - ball-striking and scrambling win Opens.
tags: Major championship links in the wind
Premium on driving control and iron flight
Scrambling and bunker play are decisive
Global field - many players have little/no PGA Tour data (picks flagged)

recentEventsUsed: the Memorial Tournament presented by Workday

trackedBets.name: Matt Fitzpatrick
trackedBets.rationale: Golf's great perfectionist, the 2022 U.S. Open champion who logs every shot he hits and sharpens his approach play to a razor's edge. A precise, wind-hardened ball-striker with a genuine links pedigree — and the form links player in the field, third at the Scottish Open last week in his home country's major. Each-way angle: the model has him about 69% to finish inside the top 8, so at 8 places (1/5 odds) the place half of the bet is where the value sits.

trackedBets.name: Viktor Hovland
trackedBets.rationale: Gaining on approach (+0.51/rd). Trending up (2.01 SG/rd over last 4 starts). Course history: strong record here - best T4 in 4 prior starts here (avg finish ~36). Links suitability: strong - best T4, avg ~34 across 9 starts at comparable links events. Did not play last week, so arrives fresh. Each-way angle: the model has him about 22% to finish inside the top 8, so at 8 places (1/5 odds) the place half of the bet is where the value sits.

trackedBets.name: Wyndham Clark
trackedBets.rationale: The hottest ball-striker in the field bar none, gaining nearly three strokes a round across his last five starts — and a U.S. Open champion whose links game has quietly matured (best finish T4, averaging around 28th across nine comparable links starts). Firm, fast ground rewards exactly his blend of power and flight control. Each-way angle: the model has him about 44% to finish inside the top 8, so at 8 places (1/5 odds) the place half of the bet is where the value sits.

trackedBets.name: Chris Gotterup
trackedBets.rationale: Golf's great closer — four wins this season already, the latest a final-round 62 from five back at the John Deere two weeks ago, and he backed it up with T11 defending his Scottish Open title last week. The links case is real: a links win on the record and a T3 in his only Open start. The one wrinkle is driving accuracy on firm, running ground with penal rough — which is why he's the small stake, not the anchor. Each-way angle: the model has him about 46% to finish inside the top 8, so at 8 places (1/5 odds) the place half of the bet is where the value sits.

trackedBets.name: Collin Morikawa
trackedBets.rationale: Gaining on approach (+0.84/rd). Trending up (1.95 SG/rd over last 3 starts). Course history: mixed record here - best T16 in 4 prior starts here (avg finish ~53). Links suitability: strong - best T4, avg ~49 across 7 starts at comparable links events. Did not play last week, so arrives fresh. Each-way angle: the model has him about 25% to finish inside the top 8, so at 8 places (1/5 odds) the place half of the bet is where the value sits.

trackedBets.name: Corey Conners
trackedBets.rationale: Gaining on approach (+0.38/rd). Steady (0.04 SG/rd over last 5 starts). Course history: solid record here - best T10 in 4 prior starts here (avg finish ~29). Links suitability: solid - best T10, avg ~34 across 9 starts at comparable links events. Did not play last week, so arrives fresh. Each-way angle: the model has him about 3% to finish inside the top 12, so at 12 places (1/5 odds) the place half of the bet is where the value sits.

trackedBets.name: Akshay Bhatia
trackedBets.rationale: The aggressive young left-hander is one of the tour's most natural scorers, and when the irons are sharp he goes very low. A short, attackable course plays straight into his birdie-making hands. Each-way angle: the model has him about 9% to finish inside the top 8, so at 8 places (1/5 odds) the place half of the bet is where the value sits.

trackedBets.name: Jesper Svensson
trackedBets.rationale: Solid all-round numbers for Royal Birkdale. Steady (0.84 SG/rd over last 2 starts). Course history: mixed record here - best T16 in 2 prior starts here (avg finish ~41). Links suitability: mixed - best T16, avg ~42 across 5 starts at comparable links events. Did not play last week, so arrives fresh. Each-way angle: a longshot flyer at 12 places (1/5 odds) — the place half is the play, taken on Tom's read rather than the model, which has limited data on him.

top10Sel.name: Si Woo Kim
top10Sel.rationale: The quiet fit for a burnt Birkdale: Si Woo Kim ranks among the tour's very best for driving accuracy and approach play — the two skills firm, running fairways pay — and he has done it at an Open before, runner-up at Royal Liverpool in 2023. T9 at the Scottish Open last week says the links game has travelled again this summer. The value: the model makes him 24% to finish top 10 where the best price implies about 19% - a +28% edge.

top10Sel.name: Xander Schauffele
top10Sel.rationale: Mr Consistency. The 2024 PGA and Open champion is the hardest man in golf to leave off a leaderboard, he turns up, contends and stacks top-10s. On a course that rewards all-round excellence, he is about as solid a top-10 play as exists. The value: the model makes him 21% to finish top 10 where the best price implies about 18% - a +15% edge.

top20Sel.name: Ludvig Åberg
top20Sel.rationale: Sweden's smooth-swinging heir apparent has looked a major champion in waiting since turning pro, a runner-up at the 2024 Masters in his very first start there. His game is built on towering, pure iron play, one of the most valuable skills in golf, and he arrives in good order off a T17 at the U.S. Open. The value: the model makes him 33% to finish top 20 where the best price implies about 23% - a +47% edge.

ewTerms: 1pt each-way at 1/5 odds, 8 places (Bet365 terms on a full-field event). Backtest sweet spot is 20/1-50/1; below ~16/1 the place return is too thin to back each-way, above ~50/1 it is a lottery ticket. Always check each book's place terms before betting.
notes: 65 players in the field have little/no PGA Tour strokes-gained data - they are excluded from value bets and flagged.

Selections are made by The Green Book — the model that ranks every player by VALUE (its probability vs the best price, the edge). Win/Top-5/Top-10/Top-20 probabilities come from a Monte Carlo simulation built on course-fit strokes-gained, recency-weighted recent form (last 8 events), trend, season class, world ranking, course history at this event, and course-conditional driving-distance and scrambling. Prices are model estimates until a live odds feed is connected.
Course history: finishes at this event over the last 4 stagings feed the model for 108 of the field (debutants are treated neutrally, not penalised).
Greens/grass: Fescue links greens and fairways. Surface type informs the course read; per-player grass-specific putting splits would need a paid feed, so it is not yet a separate player input.

Each-way to-win bets are 1pt e/w at 8 places (Bet365 terms), priced in the 20/1-50/1 backtested sweet spot. Tracked bets feed the P&L (points/units); untracked "flutters" do not. Bets settle the following week off the final leaderboard.

personalCard.note: The extras beyond the eight each-way outrights above — the bets the outright card can't hold. Corey Conners is the thread: a reliable cut-maker who goes deep, he runs through both bet builders and a 72-hole matchup against Ryan Fox. Bryson DeChambeau to miss the cut is the conviction call — three missed cuts in a row and a course that doesn't suit his game. All of it now feeds the P&L as pending. (Still watching Christiaan Bezuidenhout — in form and a good fit for firm links — though no bet is down on him.)

editorial.story: A change of plan for the season's last major: this week the board follows Tom's own card, not a straight Green Book slate. After a losing Scottish Open, the call was to trust judgment over the model — so this is a hand-built card that takes the Green Book picks worth keeping (Fitzpatrick, Clark, Gotterup, Morikawa, Hovland) and adds three of Tom's own in Corey Conners, Akshay Bhatia and Jesper Svensson, all each-way across the outright market. On top of the eight outrights there are two bet builders around the make-cut and top-30 markets, a Corey Conners v Ryan Fox 72-hole matchup, and Bryson DeChambeau to miss the cut — three straight missed cuts and a course that doesn't suit him. The Green Book's own ratings still sit on the Full Board and Field Watch tabs for context, but the card below is exactly what's been backed.
editorial.courseIntro: Royal Birkdale, and the ground is the story: a dry summer has left the fairways burnt and running, and Open week is forecast hot and sunny — up to 27°C — with an east wind gusting towards 30mph. Firm, fast and windy is the fullest links examination there is: the ball won't stop where it lands, driver becomes optional, and the Claret Jug will go to whoever controls flight and bounce for 72 holes. Birkdale's flat-bottomed dune valleys make it the fairest course on the rota — no blind luck, just relentless shot-making — and it has a habit of crowning proper champions. Jumping out to us: Matt Fitzpatrick, the form links horse in the field, and Tommy Fleetwood — Southport's own son, on the course he grew up on.
